message.content: I have a webpage I want to test programmatically for excellent support across all common web browsers.  Tersely describe the tools available for this kind of testing

addedBlocks:
## vvx
Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text:
Cross-browser web testing tools fall into a few categories:

**Local automation frameworks**
- **Playwright** — Microsoft's framework. One API drives Chromium, Firefox, and WebKit (Safari engine). Excellent auto-waiting, network mocking, trace viewer. Best default choice today.
- **Selenium WebDriver** — The veteran. Broadest language support (Java, Python, C#, JS, Ruby). Drives any browser with a WebDriver implementation. More boilerplate than Playwright.
- **Cypress** — Runs inside the browser. Great DX, but Chromium-family only for full features; Firefox supported, no Safari.
- **WebdriverIO** — Wraps WebDriver/DevTools protocols, popular for mobile + desktop combined suites.

**Cloud browser/device grids** (real browsers and OSes you don't have to install)
- **BrowserStack**, **Sauce Labs**, **LambdaTest** — Run your Playwright/Selenium suites against hundreds of OS+browser+version combos, including iOS Safari and Android Chrome on real devices.

**Static compatibility checks** (no execution)
- **caniuse.com** — Manual lookup for feature support.
- **eslint-plugin-compat** / **doiuse** (CSS) — Lint your code against a Browserslist target.
- **Browserslist** — The shared config that drives Autoprefixer, Babel, and the linters above.

**Visual regression**
- **Percy**, **Chromatic**, **Applitools** — Screenshot diffing across browsers; pairs with the automation frameworks above.

**Quick recommendation:** Playwright locally for fast feedback, then run the same tests on BrowserStack/Sauce for the long tail (Safari versions, mobile). Add Browserslist + a compat linter to catch issues before they hit a browser at all.

message.content: What do you think is most mature?  I can pick any language to interact with this automation, but I do want to really develop mature and realistic testing

content.text:
**Selenium** is the most mature by a wide margin — 20+ years old, W3C WebDriver standard, every browser vendor ships a compliant driver, every cloud grid supports it, and the ecosystem (page object patterns, reporting, CI integrations) is deeply settled. If "mature" means "boring, proven, won't surprise you in five years," Selenium wins.

**But maturity ≠ best choice today.** Playwright (since ~2020) has rapidly become the realistic default for new suites because it solves the problems that made Selenium painful: flaky waits, slow execution, awkward network interception, weak debugging. It's mature *enough* — Microsoft-backed, stable API, used at serious scale — and it produces more reliable tests with less code. The tradeoff is a younger ecosystem and a single-vendor protocol (CDP-based, with a WebKit shim) rather than a true standard.

**My recommendation for "mature and realistic":**
- **Playwright in TypeScript** as the primary suite. TS gives you the strongest editor support, the official docs lead with it, and the community examples are densest there. Python is a close second if your team prefers it — the Playwright Python bindings are first-class, not a community port.
- Run it locally for dev, then fan out to **BrowserStack or Sauce Labs** for the real Safari/iOS/old-Edge coverage you can't reproduce on your machine.
- Reach for **Selenium** only if you have a hard requirement it uniquely satisfies: ancient browsers, a language Playwright doesn't support well (Java shops sometimes), or an existing Selenium grid you must integrate with.

Realistic testing is less about the framework and more about: testing against production-like data, running on real Safari (not just "WebKit"), and having a visual-diff layer (Percy/Applitools) so you catch rendering regressions the assertions miss.

message.content: Does Playwright work as its own executive or does it 'automate' my computer's own browser installs?  Can it function 'like' different versions of chrome, edge, safari, firefox?

content.text:
**Playwright ships its own browsers.** When you install it, it downloads pinned builds of Chromium, Firefox, and WebKit into a cache directory (`~/.cache/ms-playwright` or similar). It does *not* drive your installed Chrome or Safari by default — this is deliberate, so tests are reproducible across machines and CI. Each Playwright version is locked to specific browser builds; upgrading Playwright upgrades the browsers.

**The three bundled engines:**
- **Chromium** — the open-source upstream of Chrome and Edge
- **Firefox** — a Playwright-patched build (small instrumentation patches, otherwise stock)
- **WebKit** — the engine behind Safari, built from Apple's open source

**Can it impersonate real browsers? Partially, and the distinction matters:**

- **Chrome and Edge (real, not Chromium):** Yes, genuinely. Playwright can launch your installed Google Chrome or Microsoft Edge via `channel: 'chrome'` or `channel: 'msedge'`. These are the actual shipped browsers with proprietary bits (Widevine DRM, Google services, Edge's extras) that bare Chromium lacks. This is real coverage.

- **Safari:** No, not really. Playwright's WebKit is the *engine* Safari uses, but it's not Safari. It's missing Safari's UI chrome, Apple's proprietary additions, iOS-specific quirks, and runs on Linux/Windows where Safari can't. Close enough to catch most engine-level bugs, not close enough to certify "works in Safari." For real Safari you need a Mac (local or cloud) running actual Safari via Playwright's experimental support or — more reliably — BrowserStack/Sauce.

- **Firefox:** The bundled build is essentially Firefox with test instrumentation. Realistic, though not bit-identical to the version your users run.

- **Old versions / mobile browsers:** Playwright pins one version per release. It does *not* let you test "Chrome 98" or "Safari 15." For version matrix coverage and real iOS Safari / Android Chrome, you go to a cloud grid.

**Device emulation** is a separate axis: Playwright can fake viewport, user-agent, touch, and geolocation to simulate "iPhone 14" — but that's Chromium pretending to be mobile Safari, not actual mobile Safari. Useful for layout, useless for engine bugs.

**Practical setup for serious coverage:**
1. Playwright locally with all three engines + `channel: 'chrome'` and `channel: 'msedge'` for the real Chromium-family browsers
2. BrowserStack or Sauce for actual Safari (desktop + iOS) and any version-matrix testing
3. Don't trust WebKit-on-Linux as a Safari substitute for anything user-visible

message.content: My service has an OAuth component to it: will Playwright function to exercise that?

content.text:
**Yes, Playwright handles OAuth well — it's one of its strengths.** A real browser context means the OAuth provider sees a legitimate browser doing legitimate redirects, cookies, and JS. No special protocol hacks needed.

**The standard pattern: authenticate once, reuse the session.**

OAuth flows are slow (redirects, provider login pages, sometimes MFA) and you don't want to run them before every test. Playwright's idiom:

1. A **setup project** runs once: launches a browser, completes the full OAuth flow against the real provider, then calls `context.storageState({ path: 'auth.json' })` to dump cookies + localStorage to disk.
2. Every other test loads that file via `use: { storageState: 'auth.json' }` and starts already-logged-in.

This is built in, documented, and the recommended pattern. You can have multiple storage states for multiple user roles (admin.json, viewer.json, etc.).

**The hard parts and how people handle them:**

- **Test accounts on the real provider.** Easiest path. Create dedicated test users in Google/Auth0/Okta/whatever. Works, but Google in particular gets cranky about automated logins from new IPs and may demand MFA or block the session. Provider-specific friction.

- **MFA / CAPTCHA.** These are designed to defeat automation, and they will defeat yours too. Options: (a) test accounts exempted from MFA, (b) TOTP secrets where Playwright generates the code itself (`otplib` or similar), (c) the provider's own test-mode bypass if it has one.

- **Provider's own test mode.** Auth0, Okta, Cognito, Firebase Auth, etc. usually offer a test tenant or programmatic token issuance. Faster and more reliable than driving the real login UI. Worth checking before you build UI automation.

- **Bypass entirely in some tests.** For tests that aren't *about* auth, many teams skip the OAuth dance and inject a valid session token directly — either by hitting the provider's token endpoint with client credentials, or by having the app expose a test-only login endpoint behind a flag. Faster, less flaky, but you lose coverage of the real flow.

**Realistic split:**
- **One or two end-to-end tests** that exercise the actual OAuth redirect dance, run on every CI build to catch provider/config breakage
- **Everything else** uses `storageState` (or token injection) to start logged-in and test the application itself

**Watch out for:**
- Provider rate limits if your CI hammers the login page
- Storage state expiration — refresh tokens, session cookies — your setup project should re-run when the saved state is too old
- Headless detection: some providers fingerprint headless browsers. Playwright's `headless: false` or running headless Chrome with realistic user-agent usually suffices; rarely you need stealth plugins

So: yes, Playwright will exercise your OAuth component end-to-end. Plan for `storageState` from day one and decide early which tests need the real flow vs. a shortcut.

lastPrompt: My service has an OAuth component to it: will Playwright function to exercise that?
